coredata.dc:title: Relative humidity sensor based on hollow core fiber filled with GQDs-PVA
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Yong
Tong, Rui-jie
Chen, Mao-Qing
Xia, Feng
coredata.dc:description: A novel optic fiber Fabry-Perot interferometer (FPI) based on graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and Polyvinyl Alcohol (PVA) is first proposed for relative humidity (RH) sensing and experimentally demonstrated. The GQDs-PVA compounds are filled into the hollow core fiber (HCF), which is spliced at the end of a single mode fiber (SMF). The refractive index of GQDs-PVA compounds reduces and the length of the FP cavity elongates with the increase of RH, which will lead the reflective spectrum shift to length wavelength, and the variation can characterize the change of RH values. The humidity environment is generated by different saturated saline solution, and the RH values are calibrated by a moisture meter. Experiment results reveal that the wavelength shift shows good linearity with the RH changing from 13.47%RH to 81.34%RH, and the sensitivity is 117.25 pm/%RH with the linearity relevancy of 0.9983. In addition, reversibility and repeatability experiments are carried out and the mean square deviation of six sets of data is 1.425 × 10−3, which indicates good practical development prospects. Taking the practical application into account, the influence of hydrogen and nitrogen in the air on the sensor is studied before humidity experiment, and the experiment results shows that hydrogen and nitrogen in the air have a negligible effect on the humidity sensor proposed in this paper.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fabry-Perot interferometer
RH sensor
Optical fiber sensor
Graphene quantum dots
Polyvinyl alcohol

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The combination process of PVA with GQDs and GQDs-PVA with water molecules.
Schematic diagram of the proposed FPI.
GQDs-PVA compounds: (a) just stirring evenly, (b) stand still for an hour.
The reflection spectrum of proposed FPI. Insert: spatial frequency spectrum of the sensor.
(a) Reflected FPI spectrums of the RH sensor in different hydrogen and nitrogen concentrations, (b) Fitting line between the resonance spectrum and hydrogen concentration.
Reflected spectrums of FPI at different RH values.
Fitting line between the FFT of reflected FPI spectrums and RH. Inset: The corresponding spatial spectrum.
Fitting line between the resonance spectrum and RH values.
Comparison of other optical fiber RH sensors based on different graphite materials.
Comparison of other optical fiber RH sensors.

head.ce:title: Relative humidity sensor based on hollow core fiber filled with GQDs-PVA

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Information Science and Engineering, Northeastern University, Shenyang, 110819, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Information Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel optic fiber Fabry-Perot interferometer (FPI) based on graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and Polyvinyl Alcohol (PVA) is first proposed for relative humidity (RH) sensing and experimentally demonstrated. The GQDs-PVA compounds are filled into the hollow core fiber (HCF), which is spliced at the end of a single mode fiber (SMF). The refractive index of GQDs-PVA compounds reduces and the length of the FP cavity elongates with the increase of RH, which will lead the reflective spectrum shift to length wavelength, and the variation can characterize the change of RH values. The humidity environment is generated by different saturated saline solution, and the RH values are calibrated by a moisture meter. Experiment results reveal that the wavelength shift shows good linearity with the RH changing from 13.47%RH to 81.34%RH, and the sensitivity is 117.25 pm/%RH with the linearity relevancy of 0.9983. In addition, reversibility and repeatability experiments are carried out and the mean square deviation of six sets of data is 1.425 × 10−3, which indicates good practical development prospects. Taking the practical application into account, the influence of hydrogen and nitrogen in the air on the sensor is studied before humidity experiment, and the experiment results shows that hydrogen and nitrogen in the air have a negligible effect on the humidity sensor proposed in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: Relative humidity is of great importance in various industrial areas, such as food processing industry, biopharmaceutical industry, agricultural production, civil engineering, electronic processing, air conditioning systems and so on [1–6]. Traditional electronic relative humidity (RH) monitoring devices [7–9] realize RH measurement by monitoring the changes in electrical conductivity or capacitance under different humidity. However, the complex transmission device limits the application of electronic RH sensors in remote detection or strong electromagnetic-interference environment. Fiber-optics sensing methods, with many advantages like low weigh and compact structure, electromagnetic immunity, fast response time, high accuracy, and good stability [10,11], have been considered as good candidates to overcome the previous weakness of electronic RH sensors. Nowadays, various optical fiber sensors based on different sensing structures have been developed to monitor the relative humidity. However, the pure optical fibers are not sensitive to RH. While, electronic mobility and volume of certain materials such as polymers [12], metal oxides [13] and their compounds [14] can be varied with the changing of relative humidity and have been adopted to promote the sensitivity of RH sensors. In 1997, T.E. Brook, MN. Taib et al. [15] proposed a RH sensor with Nafion-crystal coating on the surface of optical fiber and the measurement rang was changing from 40%RH to 82%RH. In 2013, Dan Su, Xueguang Qiao et al. [16] proposed a Fabry–Perot interferometer (FPI) with Polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) film coating on the end face of a single mode fiber (SMF), which obtained a sensitivity of 0.07 nm with RH measurement range changing from 7%RH to 91.2%RH. In order to improve the sensitivity, in 2013, Yanzhen Tan et al. [17] proposed a fiber tapered interferometer for RH sensing, while the sensitivity was only 97.76 pm/%RH with the RH measurement range changing from 30%RH to 90%RH. In 2016, Lei Sun et al. [18] adopted silica gel coated on the surface of microﬁber coupler (MFC), and the sensitivity increased to 1.6 nm/% RH with the RH values range changing from 70 to 86%RH. Although the sensitivity has been improved, the measurement range remained to be broaden. In 2017, Jiri Hromadka et al. [19] proposed a RH sensor with mesoporous thin ﬁlms of SiO2 NPs coating onto the surface of LPFG for RH sensing, which has the sensitivity of 0.09 ± 0.01 nm per 1%RH change 40–70%RH. At the same time, Haitao Yan, Daofu Han [20] proposed RH sensor with polyvinyl alcohol and Au coated on the grating, which is based surface plasmon resonance (SPR) principle, and the sensitivity was 0.54 nm/%RH with measurement range changing from 0 to 70%RH. Although the sensitivity and measurement range were improved, the sensing film was not easy to be coated and the thickness was difficult to control.

Different RH sensitive materials, such as PVA, agarose, and chitosan, have been applied to RH sensors. However, the absorption ability of the RH sensing materials limits the performance of the RH sensors. A new humidity sensitivity sensor with the advantages of high sensitivity, wide measurement range, compact size and easy making is needed to realize the function of high precision and wide measurement range at the same time. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), as a new zero dimension nanometer-sized graphene pieces [21], have attractive physical/chemical properties such as: the density of electron on the GQDs will decrease when GQDs adsorb water molecules [22,23], which lead to decrease of the refractive index (RI) and can be adopted to RH sensing without poisonous and harmful effect. In addition, the GQDs layers will swell after the interaction with water molecules [24] and GQDs have been applied in electrochemical humidity sensor. However, GQDs can’t combine well with optical fiber well due to the nanoscale and the properties of water solubility [25], which limit the application of GQDs in optical fiber sensors. But the hydroxyl, carboxyl and carbonyl groups on the surface of GQDs makes it easy to combine with polymers. PVA, as excellent RH sensitivity material, is able to form gels, which presents good properties of the film forming and adhesive [16]. In addition, the volume of Polyvinyl Alcohol will expand and the refractive index will reduce with the increasing of RH, which is similar to GQDs. In addition, the RI and volume of PVA and GQDs have the same variation trend with the RH changing.

According to the above discussion, a Fabry-Perot (FP) RH sensor based on GQDs-PVA compounds was firstly presented and experimentally demonstrated in this paper. The FP cavity was introduced by filling GQDs-PVA compounds into hollow core fiber (HCF). Water absorption, due to physical or chemical properties of GQDs-PVA compounds, will lead to the reflective spectrum of the proposed sensing probe shift with RH value changing. Therefore, the relationship between RH values and the spectra spectrum could be established. In order to better illustrate the performance of the RH sensor, reversibility and repeatability experiments were carried out. What’ more, the comparison with other RH optical fiber sensors was made to intuitively state the advantages of the sensor proposed in this paper. Considering practical application, the influence of nitrogen and hydrogen in the air on the RH humidity was also studied before humidity test.

GQDs-PVA compounds are considered to be a new kind of RH sensitivity materials. The hygroscopicity is the most attractive properties of GQDs-PVA. Water absorption changes with the value of RH, the combination process of PVA with GQDs and GQDs-PVA with water molecules are shown in Fig. 1 .

The absorbed water molecules will fill the slices of GQDs, what’s more, the interspace between GQDs and PVA layers will also be filled by water molecules due to the reticular structure of GQDs and PVA. Meanwhile, the adsorbing of water molecules will lead to the change of electron on the surface of GQDs-PVA, which leads to the change of refractive index.
Fig. 2 presents the schematic diagram of the proposed FPI based on GQDs-PVA compounds.

The FPI is manufactured by filling the GQDs-PVA compounds to the HCF which is spliced to the end of a section of SMF. The fiber core of HCF is used to hold the GQDs-PVA compounds and form a Fabry-Perot interferometer to measure RH. GQDs-PVA compounds will swell with the increase of RH. The air cavity is generated by incomplete filling to prevent the GQDs-PVA compounds overflow the hollow core fiber by excessive expanse toward the hollow core fiber, which will damage the sensor. When the incident light propagates through the HCF and GQDs-PVA compounds, three reflective interfaces appear, where a micro-cavity and Fresnel reflection are formed. The interference intensity of the output light can be expressed as follows:
(1)
I =
I 1
+
I 2
+
I 3
− 2
I 1
I 2
cos (
ϕ 1
) − 2
I 2
I 3
cos (
ϕ 2
) − 2
I 1
I 3
cos (
ϕ 1
+
ϕ 2
)
Where
I 1
,
I 2
,and
I 3
represent optical intensity of the three reflected faces, respectively, and
ϕ 1
and
ϕ 2
represent phase values induced by the air cavity of the HCF and the GQDs-PVA compounds
(2)
ϕ 1
=
4 π
n
a i r
L 1
λ
ϕ 2
=
4 π
n
G Q D s − P V A
L 2
λ
Where
n
a i r
and
n
G Q D s − P V A
are the refractive indices of the air cavity and the GQDs-PVA compounds respectively. The air cavity of the HCF is sealed and
n
a i r
is changeless with the change of RH. While GQDs-PVA compounds will swell with the change of RH and
L 2
will enlarge in two direction of the left and the right. The variation of
L 1
is caused by
L 2
. In the filling process, the HCF is almost fully filled with GQDs-PVA compounds, so the effect of
L 1
can be negligible. Therefore, the change of
ϕ 1
is insignificant and can be ignored, and the interference spectrum is mainly determined by GQDs-PVA compounds. Therefore, the wavelength of the dip can be described as:
(3)
λ n
=
4 π
n
G Q D s − P V A
L 2
θ
(4)
θ = 2 k π −
π 2
+ arctan
I 3
+
I 2
cos (
ϕ 2
)
I 2
sin (
ϕ 2
)
Where
λ n
represents the location of interference valley of FPI and θ is the corresponding phase value, k is an integer. Eq. (3) indicates that the resonance wavelength are determined by RI and the length of GQDs-PVA compounds.
n
G Q D s − P V A
and
L 1
changes with RH, therefore, the relationship between
λ n
and RH is established

Before fabricating the FPI, two sections of the ordinary SMF (Corning) with the diameter of 8/125 μm (core/outer clad) and HCF with the diameter of 50/125 μm (hole/outer clad) were prepared. Firstly, the two sections of HCF and SMF were spliced together by commercial fusion splicer (FITEL S178), which was operated by manual mode, and then the HCF was cut off to get dozens of microns long HCF, and then is the GQDs-PVA making and filling process, the specific process is shown in Fig. 3 :

(1) Adding 83.3
m g
PVA to the beaker firstly, and then 0.2
m L
GQDs solution with the concentration of 1
m g / m L
was added. (2) Stirring the mixture of PVA and GQDs with a magnetic stirrer for an hour to make sure the GQDs-PVA was well-mixed. The result was shown in Fig. 4 (a). However, there were many bubbles in GQDs-PVA compounds, which will introduce uneven distribution of the membrane and influence performance of the sensor. In order to remove the bubble, the GQDs-PVA compounds were put at 25 °C for standing in one hour, and the GQDs-PVA compounds without bubbles were shown in Fig. 4(b). (3) Filling the PVA/GQDs to the HCF by using the dip coating machine. The sensing structure without any filling was fixed on the dip coating machine, and then lowered the sensing structure. The distance between the sensor probe and the GQDs-PVA compounds was monitoring by optical spectrum analyzer (OSA). The interference spectrum will appear on the OSA when the fiber tip touched the GQDs-PVA membrane, and then slow down the lowering speed. The filling process is finished when the spectrum does not change with the lowering of the sensing probe. (4) After filling process, the sensing probe was put indoor at 25 °C for four hours to remove part of the water molecules, which will reduce the response time of experiment.

Fig. 5 presents the reflection spectrum of proposed RH sensor, the obvious interference fringe shows that the sensor is successful made, and the fringe contrast and the free spectral range (FSR) are 6.37 dB and 16.19 nm, respectively.

The inset in Fig. 5 is spatial frequency spectrum of the reflection spectrum acquired by Fast Fourier Transformation (FFT), which shows that there are one strong peak and one weak peak, where the strong peak value is about eightfold greater than that of weak peak, which indicates the interference spectrum is mainly determined by the strong peak and verifies that only one FP interference plays a major role in the sensor probe.

The experiments were executed at 25 °C, and Fig. 6 explains the propagation process of input light: Broadband (from amplified spontaneous emission (ASE)) propagated the RH sensitive probe, then the output light was tested by OSA with spectral resolution of 0.02 nm. Different relative humidity environment was generated by filling plastic bottles with different saturated saline solutions, and the saline solutions are lithium chloride, magnesium chloride, magnesium nitrate, sodium bromide, sodium chloride and potassium chloride six saturated salt solutions, respectively. To generate different RH values, a hygrometer to record the RH valued in plastic bottles, and the hygrometer was applied by ROTRONIC brand. In order to guarantee the accuracy of measurement, the RH sensitive sensor probe and the probe of hygrometer were put at the same horizontal position in the plastic bottles. The direct current voltage source (DCVS) provides energy for the hygrometer.

Before relative humidity experiment, whether the sensor will respond to gases in the air is tested. Z. S. Hosseini and his groups [26] have proved an electrochemical humidity sensor based on GQDs responds weakly to hydrogen, and there is no considerable response to carbon dioxide, carbon monoxide and methane, which means the carbon dioxide, carbon monoxide and methane almost don’t react with GQDs.

According to the chemical bond bonding principle, these gases don’t react with PVA. Therefore, carbon dioxide, carbon monoxide and methane have no effect on the RH sensor we proposed. In order to explore whether hydrogen and nitrogen in the air have effect on the sensing performance of the sensor proposed in this paper, the sensor probe was placed in pure nitrogen, 0.2% hydrogen and 99.8% nitrogen, 0.4% hydrogen and 99.6% nitrogen, 0.6% hydrogen and 99.4% nitrogen, 0.8% hydrogen and 99.2% nitrogen and 1% hydrogen and 99% nitrogen at 25 °C, respectively. The results are shown in Fig. 7 . Fig. 7 (a) is the reflected FPI spectrums of the RH sensor in different hydrogen and nitrogen concentrations, Fig. 7 (b) is fitting line between the resonance spectrum and hydrogen concentration, in which the maximum error is 0.083 nm, which equivalents to the wavelength shift caused by 0.7%RH. Therefore, the small fluctuate can be neglected. Besides, in our experiment, the resolution of OSA is 0.02 nm, so this fluctuate may be generated by the reading error. In a word, it reveals that the interference spectrum almost has no shift, which means the sensor has almost no respond to the hydrogen and nitrogen in the air.
The following is RH experiments results which is tested at 25 °C. The resonance spectrums of FPI are shown in Fig. 8 .

It showed that the reflected spectrums of FPI shifted to long wavelength direction with the increasing of the RH values, and
λ n
increased with the increase of RH, which verified the FPI informed by GQDs-PVA compounds played the main role in the interferometer, which means that the change of air cavity introduced by incomplete filling is mainly depend on GQDs-PVA compounds. In addition, the spatial frequency f can be expressed as:
(5)
f =
1
F S R
=
n
G Q D s − P V A
L 2
λ n 2
Where FSR is free spectral range. It showed that the relationship between the spatial frequency f and
λ n 2
was inversely proportion, while
n
G Q D s − P V A
and
L 2
were in direct proportion to f In addition, Fig. 9 showed the relationship between the FFT of reflected FPI spectrums and RH

Fig. 9 indicated that the frequency was changeless with the change of RH. Combined with Eq. (5), the increase of
λ n 2
and the decrease of
n
G Q D s − P V A
leaded to the decrease of the f , while Fig. 9 showed changeless of FSR, which verified that the change of
L 2
plays the main role in RH sensor. In conclusion, the red shift of the reflected FPI spectrums was mainly due to the swelling of GQDs-PVA compounds

The linear fitting curve of experiment data between wavelength and RH values was shown in Fig. 10 . It revealed the sensitivity was 117.25 pm/%RH in the range of 13.47%RH to 81.34%RH, meanwhile, it maintains good linearity relevancy of 0.9983 and resolution is 0.171%RH, which is better than that in Ref. [16]. The error bars indicates the measurement results are disturbed, which is mainly due to the interferences of surrounding temperature, vibration, and the concentration error of measured RH.

However, the sensitivity and linearity are not the only scanty evaluation criteria, repeatability and reversibility are also the major objects of investigation. Fig. 11 shows the experiment results with RH values changing from low to high and then from high to low.

The black and the red sampling points in Fig. 11 represents the increasing and decreasing process of RH values, respectively, which illustrates the resonant wavelength at the same RH value point in the increasing and decreasing process of RH values is almost the same. The linear correlations of black line and red line are 99.83% and 99.015%, respectively. On account of the wavelength resolution of OSA (0.02 nm), the RH resolution is estimated to be 0.0023 with the 0.117 nm/%RH sensitivity in the RH measurement range. It shows that the sampling points in the process of rising and falling have high overlap, which reveals the sensor we proposed presents good reversibility.

In addition, three times repetitive experiment results with the RH values changing from low to high and then from high to low are shown in Fig. 12 . There are slight disturbances in repetitive experiments, which possibly be caused by the fluctuation of light source. What’s more, considering the data gathering process, the fluctuation may be resulted by asynchrony of data gathering and recording process. However, the mean square deviation of the six sets of data is
1.425 ×
10
− 3
, which demonstrates that the FPI based on GQDs-PVA for RH sensing has high sensitivity and good repeatability.
The comparison of RH sensors based on optical fiber coated with different graphite materials are shown in Table 1 .

The comparison in Table 1 reveals that the proposed humidity sensor in this paper has a wide RH range than those presented in [28–32], and similar to Ref. [27]. While the correlation coefficient in this paper is higher than those depicted in [27,29–32]. The sensitivity of Ref [28] is the highest, while the measurement range is only from 85 to 97.6%RH, which is too narrow for practical application.

Considering the present measurement methods, RH sensors based on optical fiber can be divided into four major types: FBG-based, LPFG-based, SPR-based and interferometer-based RH sensors. In addition, the comparison of the four types of RH sensor is shown in Table 2 .

FBG-based sensors with refractive index sensing probes are suitable for remote measurement and distributed monitoring due to the endemic multiplex property; However, the sensitivity is not high, and the thermal sensitivity is the intrinsic of FBG which is not appropriate for practical application; Although the LPFG-based sensors have a wide measurement range, high sensitivity only appears in the range of 0˜0.8%RH, which limits the engineering application background. In addition, specific equipment is required to insert grating on the ordinary optical fiber. Although SPR-based RH sensors present high sensitivity, high precision coating instruments are needed during the making process of sensor probes. Interferometer-based RH sensors, mainly based on double beam interference principle, via flexible structures and sensing materials to improve the sensitivity and measurement range. Compared with other interferometers, FP interferometers have the advantages of compact size, easy making and high sensitivity. The RH sensor based on FP interferometer we proposed in this paper combines high sensitivity and wide measurement range together, the sensitivity is about twice as high as the FP interferometer based on polyvinyl alcohol. In addition, the good linearity shows the possibility to a wider range of measurement.

In conclusion, a new reflective FPI based on GQDs-PVA is first put forward for relative humidity in this work. The sensing principle has been confirmed by theoretical analysis and experiments. Before the humidity test, the effect of gases in the air was studied which reveal that hydrogen and nitrogen almost have almost no effect on the RH sensor, which means the RH sensor has good potential for practical application. Humidity experimental results show good linearity in the range of 13.47%RH to 81.34%RH with the sensitivity of 117.25 pm/%RH, and the linear correlation coefficient is 99.83%. The comparing with the existing fiber-optic RH sensors based on graphite materials shows that the FPI based on GQDs-PVA combine the advantages of high sensitivity and wide measurement range together. In addition, RH optical fiber sensor based on GQDs-PVA has higher correlation coefficient than those based on graphite materials. Meanwhile, the comparing with fiber humidity sensors based on other principles indicates that the FPI based on GQDs-PVA has many advantages such as easy making and high sensitivity. What’s more, the good reversibility and repeatability give it more research potential in practical application.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported in part by the National Natural Science Foundation of China under Grant 61425003 and 61773102, the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities under Grant N160408001, N170407005 and part by the State Key Laboratory of Synthetical Automation for Process Industries under Grant 2013ZCX09.
